food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dish with egg and spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Masala Kalaki. Mapped to closest parent 'fried rice food product' (FOODON_00002809) as this dish is a spiced egg-fried rice preparation.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FAN (Fried rice) used as closest parent. No specific code exists for Masala Kalaki or Malaysian Indian egg fried rice in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for Masala Kalaki or its closest parent category in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Masala Kalaki typically weighs approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of spiced egg-fried rice served at a Malaysian Indian or Mamak stall.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng (Fried Rice with Egg) — adapted with Indian spice profile; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central: Fried Rice with Egg (SR Legacy) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD composite estimate for egg fried rice
nutrient_source.organization: IMR Malaysia MyFCD / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Masala Kalaki exists in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA SR Legacy. Values are adapted from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for Nasi Goreng (egg fried rice) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fried rice with egg. The Indian spice additions (turmeric, chilli, cumin, curry leaves) contribute negligible macronutrient change at typical quantities. Per-100g values represent a composite estimate for this dish category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and egg fried rice preparations — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base, GI ~64)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Masala Kalaki. GI of 64 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice-based fried rice preparations (GI range 56–72, representative value 64). The addition of egg and spices does not substantially alter the glycemic response of the rice base. GL per serving calculated as (64 × 25.4 × 3.0) / 100 = 48.8 g per 300 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~960 mg per standard 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt and soy sauce during preparation, as a full serving can contribute approximately 40–48% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~64) and high glycemic load per serving (~48.8 g GL for 300 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Masala Kalaki in reduced portions (100–150 g) and pair it with non-starchy vegetables or a protein side to blunt the postprandial glucose response driven by the white rice base.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Egg-derived cholesterol (~95 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium; saturated fat is relatively low at 1.2 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should moderate intake frequency due to the cholesterol contribution from egg and the sodium load, while noting that the saturated fat content is modest and the use of vegetable oil limits trans fat exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala Kalaki is a Malaysian Indian spiced egg fried rice dish popular at Mamak stalls and Indian Muslim restaurants in Malaysia and Singapore. The dish is prepared by stir-frying pre-cooked white rice in a wok with beaten eggs, onions, tomatoes, green chillies, curry leaves, cumin seeds, turmeric, chilli powder, and garam masala in hot oil. The eggs are scrambled directly into the rice during cooking, creating a moist, fragrant, and lightly spiced one-dish meal. The texture is soft and slightly sticky from the egg coating, with aromatic bursts from the whole spices and curry leaves. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and warmly spiced with South Indian masala notes. Masala Kalaki is typically served as a budget-friendly breakfast or supper item, priced between SGD 3.50 and SGD 4.00 in Singapore. Nutritionally, it provides moderate energy (~172 kcal per 100 g), a useful protein contribution from egg (~5.2 g per 100 g), and moderate carbohydrate from white rice (~25.4 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is moderate to high (~320 mg per 100 g), relevant for hypertension management. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal, making it accessible to a wide range of dietary preferences in the Singaporean and Malaysian context.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Masala Kalaki exists in any approved primary source (IMR Malaysia MyFCD, HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA). Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD Nasi Goreng (egg fried rice) entries and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy fried rice with egg. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice-based fried rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on IMR Malaysia MyFCD Nasi Goreng (egg fried rice) and USDA SR Legacy fried rice with egg; no exact Masala Kalaki entry was found in any approved primary source. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this adaptation. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries used. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries used. 5. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as this dish is prepared with vegetable oil and contains no hydrogenated fat ingredients; confirmed zero by formulation analogy. 6. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice-based fried rice; no direct GI measurement for Masala Kalaki was found. 7. GL per serving = (64 × 25.4 × 3.0) / 100 = 48.8 g for a 300 g serving. 8. The dish is classified as Malaysian cuisine with strong Malaysian Indian (Mamak) cultural identity; multilingual names reflect Singaporean context per G6 rules. 9. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for this dish.
